Not to be confused with [Indian people](/wiki/Indian_people "Indian people").

**Indo-Aryan peoples** (also known as **Indic peoples** in the context of [Indo-European studies](/wiki/Indo-European_studies "Indo-European studies")) are a diverse collection of [peoples](/wiki/People "People") predominantly found in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), who (traditionally) speak [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"). Historically, [Aryans](/wiki/Aryan "Aryan") were the [pastoralists](/wiki/Pastoralism "Pastoralism") who spoke Indo-Iranian languages, [migrated](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_migrations "Indo-Aryan migrations") from [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") into South Asia, and introduced the [Proto-Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Aryan_language "Proto-Indo-Aryan language").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErdosy2012-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The early [Indo-Aryan peoples](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_migrations "Indo-Aryan migrations") were known to be closely related to the [Iranian](/wiki/Iranian_peoples "Iranian peoples") group that have resided west of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") on the [Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau"); an evident connection in cultural, linguistic, and historical ties. Today, the majority of Indo-Aryan speakers are found south of [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") and east of the Indus,[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Disputed statement") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Indo-Aryan_peoples#Dubious "Talk:Indo-Aryan peoples")*] across the modern-day regions of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives") and northern half of [India](/wiki/India "India") as well as parts of [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") ([Kunar](/wiki/Kunar_languages "Kunar languages")).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indo-Aryan_peoples&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Proto-Indo-Iranians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indo-Aryan_peoples&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Proto-Indo-Iranians")]

Main articles: [Indo-Iranians](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians"), [Proto-Indo-Europeans](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans"), [Aryan](/wiki/Aryan "Aryan"), [Indo-European migrations](/wiki/Indo-European_migrations "Indo-European migrations"), and [Indo-Aryan migrations](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_migrations "Indo-Aryan migrations")

Further information: [Genetics and archaeogenetics of South Asia](/wiki/Genetics_and_archaeogenetics_of_South_Asia "Genetics and archaeogenetics of South Asia") and [Peopling of India](/wiki/Peopling_of_India "Peopling of India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Indo-Iranian_origins.png/250px-Indo-Iranian_origins.png)](/wiki/File:Indo-Iranian_origins.png)

Archaeological cultures associated with [Indo-Iranian migrations](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") (after [EIEC](/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Indo-European_Culture "Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture")). The [Andronovo](/wiki/Andronovo "Andronovo"), [BMAC](/wiki/Bactria%E2%80%93Margiana_Archaeological_Complex "Bactria–Margiana Archaeological Complex") and [Yaz cultures](/wiki/Yaz_culture "Yaz culture") have often been associated with Indo-Iranian migrations. The [GGC](/wiki/Gandhara_grave_culture "Gandhara grave culture"), [Cemetery H](/wiki/Cemetery_H "Cemetery H"), [Copper Hoard](/wiki/Copper_Hoard_culture "Copper Hoard culture"), [OCP](/wiki/Ochre_Coloured_Pottery_culture "Ochre Coloured Pottery culture"), and [PGW](/wiki/Painted_Grey_Ware_culture "Painted Grey Ware culture") cultures are candidates for cultures associated with [Indo-Aryan migrations](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_migration "Indo-Aryan migration").

The introduction of the [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") in the Indian subcontinent was the outcome of a [migration of Indo-Aryan people](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_migrations "Indo-Aryan migrations") from Central Asia into the northern [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") (modern-day [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka")). Another group of Indo-Aryans migrated further westward and founded the [Mitanni](/wiki/Mitanni "Mitanni") kingdom in northern Syria[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007454-11) (c. 1500–1300 BC); the other group was the Vedic people.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith200933_note_20-12) According to [Christopher I. Beckwith](/wiki/Christopher_I._Beckwith "Christopher I. Beckwith"), the [Wusun](/wiki/Wusun "Wusun") people of [Inner Asia](/wiki/Inner_Asia "Inner Asia") in [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") could have been of Indo-Aryan origin.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckwith2009376-13)

The [Proto-Indo-Iranians](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians"), from which the Indo-Aryans developed, are identified with the [Sintashta culture](/wiki/Sintashta_culture "Sintashta culture") (2100–1800 BCE),[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007390_(fig._15.9),_405–411-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuz'mina2007222-15) and the [Andronovo culture](/wiki/Andronovo_culture "Andronovo culture"),[[16]](#cite_note-Smith-16) which flourished ca. 1800–1400 BCE in the steppes around the [Aral Sea](/wiki/Aral_Sea "Aral Sea"), present-day Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan. The Proto-Indo-Aryan split off around 1800–1600 BCE from the Iranians,[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007408-17) moved south through the [Bactria-Margiana Culture](/wiki/Bactria%E2%80%93Margiana_Archaeological_Complex "Bactria–Margiana Archaeological Complex"), south of the Andronovo culture, borrowing some of their distinctive religious beliefs and practices from the BMAC, and then migrated further south into the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") and north-western India.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007-5) The migration of the Indo-Aryans was part of the larger diffusion of [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") from the [Proto-Indo-European homeland](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_homeland "Proto-Indo-European homeland") at the [Pontic–Caspian steppe](/wiki/Pontic%E2%80%93Caspian_steppe "Pontic–Caspian steppe") which started in the 4th millennium BCE.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnthony2007-5)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) The [GGC](/wiki/Gandhara_grave_culture "Gandhara grave culture"), [Cemetery H](/wiki/Cemetery_H "Cemetery H"), [Copper Hoard](/wiki/Copper_Hoard_culture "Copper Hoard culture"), [OCP](/wiki/Ochre_Coloured_Pottery_culture "Ochre Coloured Pottery culture"), and [PGW](/wiki/Painted_Grey_Ware_culture "Painted Grey Ware culture") cultures are candidates for cultures associated with Indo-Aryans.

The Indo-Aryans were united by shared cultural norms and language, referred to as *aryā* 'noble'. Over the last four millennia, the Indo-Aryan culture has evolved particularly inside India itself, but its origins are in the conflation of values and heritage of the Indo-Aryan and indigenous people groups of India.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Diffusion of this culture and language took place by patron-client systems, which allowed for the absorption and acculturation of other groups into this culture, and explains the strong influence on other cultures with which it interacted.

Genetically, most Indo-Aryan-speaking populations are descendants of a mix of Central Asian steppe pastoralists, Iranian hunter-gatherers, and, to a lesser extent, South Asian hunter-gatherers—commonly known as Ancient Ancestral South Indians (AASI). Dravidians are descendants of a mix of South Asian hunter-gatherers and Iranian hunter-gatherers, and to a lesser extent, Central Asian steppe pastoralists. South Indian Tribal Dravidians descend majorly from South Asian hunter-gatherers, and to a lesser extent Iranian hunter-gatherers.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReich_et_al.2009-22)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENarasimhan_et_al.2019-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Yelmen_2019-24) Additionally, Austroasiatic and Tibeto-Burmese speaking people contributed to the genetic make-up of South Asia.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBasu_et_al.2016-25)

[Indigenous Aryanism](/wiki/Indigenous_Aryanism "Indigenous Aryanism") propagates the idea that the Indo-Aryans were indigenous to the Indian subcontinent, and that the Indo-European languages spread from there to central Asia and Europe. Contemporary support for this idea is ideologically driven, and has no basis in objective data and mainstream scholarship.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel200195-26)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison2006-27)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuha2007341-28)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosse2005438-29)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlson2016136-30)
